title: Dog Crusty Ear Tips: Causes, Triage, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what causes crusty scabs on your dog's ear tips, the urgency level for vet care, and why photos are vital for a clear diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Crusty scabs on a dog's ear tips are usually non-emergency issues caused by pests, allergies, or inflammation, but you should see a vet if they bleed heavily. Schedule a routine appointment within a few days to determine the exact cause and begin treatment.

Why does my dog have crusty scabs on the edges of their ears?

Finding crusty scabs on the edges of your dog's ears can be worrying for any pet parent. These crusts are often the result of conditions like fly strike (where biting flies target the thin skin of the ears), ear margin dermatosis, or vasculitis, which is an inflammation of the blood vessels. In some cases, environmental allergies or localized infections can also cause the skin to thicken and scab over.

Are crusty ear tips on my dog considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for crusty ear tips is typically Low, as these lesions are rarely life-threatening.
  • Schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian within the next few days.
  • Seek care sooner if the ears are actively bleeding heavily, or if your dog seems extremely lethargic or painful.

How does taking a photo of my dog's ears help the veterinarian?

  • Taking a clear, well-lit photograph of your dog's ear tips is an excellent triage step.
  • High-resolution images allow your veterinarian to see the exact pattern of the scabbing and monitor if the lesions are spreading or changing over time.
  • Photos are especially helpful if the appearance of the ears fluctuates throughout the day, providing a stable record for your vet to analyze during the consultation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Primary causes of otitis externa, which can manifest as crusty ear tips, include allergy, autoimmune diseases (e.g., pemphigus), endocrine disorders, epithelialization disorders, foreign bodies, glandular disorders, immune-mediated reactions (e.g., drug reactions), fungal infections (e.g., aspergillosis), parasites, and viral infections (e.g., canine distemper). Ear margin seborrhea, common in Dachshunds and other breeds with pendulous pinnae, can cause waxy, gray to yellow scales adherent to the base of hair shafts on the ear margins; in severe cases, the ear margins are edematous and fissured. Differential diagnoses include sarcoptic mange, pinnal alopecia, and proliferative conditions.

Chapter: Dermatology, Neurology, Diagnostic Imaging

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 529)

faqs.question: Should I pick the scabs off my dog's ears?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid picking the scabs. This can cause pain, bleeding, and potential secondary infections. It is best to let a veterinarian examine them first.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter creams on the scabs?
faqs.answer: Do not apply any ointments or creams until your vet has diagnosed the cause. Some human medications can be toxic or irritating to canine skin.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to ear margin issues?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds with thin ear leather, such as Dachshunds, Greyhounds, and Whippets, are more frequently affected by ear margin dermatosis and vasculitis.
